title: SEGMENT 11 — Ginkgo Bioworks and the strain-engineering factory

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Ginkgo Bioworks and the strain-engineering factory

If Amyris was the bet that you could make a particular product family — terpenoids — at industrial scale, Ginkgo Bioworks is the bet that the most valuable thing about metabolic engineering is the strain engineering platform itself, not any one product.
Ginkgo was founded in 2008 in Boston by Jason Kelly, Reshma Shetty, Barry Canton, Tom Knight, and Austin Che, several of them out of Tom Knight's MIT lab.
Tom Knight — you may remember from the genetic circuits lecture, he was a key figure in the BioBricks and standardized parts era — provided much of the intellectual scaffolding.
The pitch was unusual.
Ginkgo would not sell a particular molecule.

Ginkgo would sell the service of engineering microbes — strains — for whatever customer wanted them, across an enormous breadth of industries: food, fragrance, pharmaceutical, agricultural, materials, cosmetic, anything where an engineered microbe could be the production platform.
The architecture they built — what they call the Foundry — is essentially an industrial-scale automated lab.

Robotic liquid handlers, DNA synthesis pipelines, microfluidic screening, mass spectrometry stations, fermentation banks, the works, all wired together with software so that a single project manager can run many strain engineering campaigns in parallel.
The Foundry runs continuously.

Each engineering campaign cycles through the design-build-test-learn loop — design the genetic changes, build the strains, test their performance, learn from the results, repeat — many times per project, far faster than any individual academic lab could manage.

The deal with customers is usually structured so that the customer brings the target molecule and the market knowledge; Ginkgo brings the engineering and gets paid in some combination of upfront fees, milestone payments, royalties, and equity.
Customer takes the patent on the resulting strain or molecule; Ginkgo takes the upside.
The portfolio is enormous and intentionally horizontal.
Ginkgo has worked on engineered yeasts to produce specialty fragrance ingredients for perfume companies.
They have worked on engineered microbes for animal-free dairy proteins.
They have worked on engineered nitrogen-fixing microbes for sustainable agriculture, in partnership with Bayer through their joint venture Joyn Bio.
They have worked on engineered microbes for various pharmaceutical and nutritional products.
They have done CRO work for the cannabis industry.
They have acquired smaller companies — Bayer's bioscience unit, Zymergen — to consolidate the strain engineering market.
The breadth, more than the depth in any one product, is the strategic bet.

The analogy I want you to carry: Ginkgo Bioworks is the strain-engineering factory — a horizontal pipeline that works for clients across food, fragrance, pharma, agriculture, materials, with the promise: we engineer the strains, you take the patent, we take royalties or fees.
The product is engineering capacity itself, not any specific molecule.
That model has been controversial in industry circles.
Critics ask whether engineering services without owned IP can sustain the valuations Ginkgo has historically commanded.

Defenders argue that the platform — once built at sufficient scale — has economies of scale that no in-house corporate engineering effort can match, and that the cumulative knowledge across many campaigns becomes its own competitive moat.

The financial history has been turbulent, with Ginkgo going through significant valuation compression after going public via SPAC in 2021, but the platform itself continues to operate and to take on engineering projects.

The reason I want you to know about Ginkgo, regardless of how the company's financial story ultimately plays out, is that the platform model they pioneered is now the dominant structure for ambitious metabolic engineering in industry.
The old model — found a company around a single molecule, raise capital, scale it, sell it — turned out to be very risky, because the molecule market often did not cooperate (cf.
Amyris and biofuels).

The new model — build engineering capacity, find many smaller markets in parallel, hedge across them — spreads the risk and exploits the fact that strain engineering tools developed for one project are largely reusable for the next.
Many other companies have adopted variants of this model.

Some focus on particular product verticals — Perfect Day for dairy proteins, Geltor for collagen — but rely on contract strain engineering services from companies like Ginkgo for parts of their pipeline.

The biomanufacturing industry has restructured itself, in the 2010s and 2020s, around the recognition that the limit on the field is not ideas but throughput, and throughput is built in places like the Foundry.
There is one more cultural point worth making.
Ginkgo, more than any other company in the space, has been explicit about treating biology as an engineering discipline rather than a science.
They hire industrial engineers, not just biologists.
They publish less in academic journals than a typical company of their size and more in trade and industry venues.
They treat the strain engineering process as a manufacturing process to be optimized for cost, throughput, and reliability.

That stance — biology as engineering, not biology as discovery — is contentious among traditional biologists but has been broadly influential in how the next generation of biotech companies organize themselves.
Whether the analogy fully holds up depends on whether biology can ever really be made as predictable as silicon, which we discussed at length in the genetic circuits lecture.
The bet, at Ginkgo and at companies that have followed its lead, is that the gap is closing fast enough to support a real industry.

Now let us turn to one of the biggest application areas of the modern metabolic engineering wave: plant-derived natural products, particularly the drugs that come from rare plants and have historically been supply-constrained.
